items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.

items.q: Which component handles visual and spatial information?
items.why: Students mix up the slave systems. The phonological loop is for auditory/verbal information; the sketchpad is for visual/spatial. The central executive coordinates but doesn't store.

items.q: When is deception in research ever acceptable?
items.correct: When demand characteristics would otherwise invalidate the study, with thorough debrief and ethics approval.
items.why: Students think BPS guidelines forbid deception outright. They actually permit it when justified, minimised, and followed by full debrief allowing withdrawal of data.

items.q: Maguire (2000) found enlarged ___ in London taxi drivers:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Hippocampi. “Amygdala” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's 5-HTT and depression. “DRD4 and risk-taking” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Maguire publish her London taxi drivers study?
items.why: The real figure is 2000 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
